episode_meta.title: Upasna Gautam: The Product Manager's Guide to AI in News

ai_tools: Google Docs (mentioned as analogy with AI enhancements)
Microsoft Word (mentioned as analogy with AI enhancements)

use_cases.description: Automating content tagging to reduce repetitive manual work for journalists
use_cases.outcome: Streamlined workflows so journalists can focus on creativity, strategy, and reporting
Faster and more efficient publishing processes
Faster and more efficient publishing processes

use_cases.description: Data integration into editorial workflow - surfacing dashboards showing story performance with actionable insights such as suggesting better headlines or identifying high-performing story formats in real time
use_cases.outcome: Equip journalists to make decisions that are both fast and informed

use_cases.description: Content modularity - reusing assets like videos, text blocks, images to scale content creation across platforms

Using ChatGPT to draft initial versions of documents, synthesize large amounts of information, explore alternative approaches to a problem, and translate technical documentation into executive summaries
use_cases.outcome: Reduced time spent on repetitive or lower-value tasks, enhanced productivity, rapid iteration

use_cases.description: Using ChatGPT to reframe communication and tailor messages for diverse audiences across hundreds of global stakeholders, and draft templates for operational processes
use_cases.outcome: Ability to operate at scale with personalized communication

key_claims.claim: AI isn't going to take you from zero to one - you have to have a strong foundation to build upon
key_claims.speaker: Upasna Gautam

key_claims.claim: Not every inefficiency warrants an AI solution
key_claims.speaker: Upasna Gautam

key_claims.claim: CNN's current staff of product, technology, and design is about 350 to 400 people
key_claims.speaker: Upasna Gautam

key_claims.claim: CNN ran a presidential election on the new Stellar platform with zero critical incidents
key_claims.speaker: Upasna Gautam

key_claims.claim: There is a blind race in the news industry to adopt AI without understanding the problem being solved
key_claims.speaker: Upasna Gautam

key_claims.claim: If you're a good writer or a good coder, AI will make you even better; if you're bad, it will make you even worse
key_claims.speaker: Upasna Gautam

key_claims.claim: The journalism industry has eroding trust over the past couple of years and cannot attract top tech talent as easily as other industries
key_claims.speaker: Upasna Gautam

key_claims.claim: AI can easily amplify misinformation if not carefully managed
key_claims.speaker: Upasna Gautam

key_claims.claim: CNN deploys changes to production every single week through constant shipping, testing, and iterating
key_claims.speaker: Upasna Gautam

challenges.description: Asking the right questions to solve the right problems - tendency to skip product discovery and jump to solutions, especially with AI shiny object syndrome
Balancing speed with trust - AI can accelerate content creation but also amplifies misinformation if not carefully managed; need for robust safeguards, guardrails, and ethical frameworks
Inequality of access to AI tools - under-resourced newsrooms cannot adopt as quickly due to lack of resources, knowledge, or information
Editorial teams fear AI might erode journalistic values, replace human judgment, or take their jobs
Centralization of dispersed data - massive collections of valuable information about audiences and teams scattered across systems in larger newsrooms
Inability to attract top tech talent to the journalism industry compared to other industries
Migrating from legacy CMS to new platform required almost a year of training and onboarding, going team by team with different nuanced needs

quotes.text: AI isn't going to take you ever from zero to one. You have to have a strong foundation to build upon.
quotes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

quotes.text: Not every inefficiency warrants an AI solution. And this is not just AI related. I think this is pro AI. especially now because it's shiny object syndrome around it. But in general, when it comes to product development, we tend to skip over the product discovery process and immediately start making assumptions to create a solution.
quotes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

quotes.text: There is this sometimes blind race to just adopt without understanding the problem that's actually being solved.
quotes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

quotes.text: If you're a good writer or a good coder, it will make you an even better writer or an even better coder. But if you suck at writing and you're a bad coder, it will make you even worse.
quotes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

quotes.text: The foundational work, the human work of identifying the right problems to solve, asking the right questions, setting the scale clear purpose-driven vision. Like you can't outsource that stuff to AI.
quotes.speaker: Upasna Gautam
quotes.topic: Limits of AI in product management

quotes.text: Involving editorial teams early in the process, whether they're your customer or not, and co-designing solutions and gathering feedback with them. It's like the ultimate product management hack in news.
quotes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

recommendations.advice: Deeply understand user pain points before proposing AI solutions - talk to users in depth, observe workflows, conduct interviews, identify bottlenecks
recommendations.speaker: Upasna Gautam

recommendations.advice: Position AI as an enabler, not a replacement - demonstrate how it eliminates friction and frees journalists to focus on high-value work
recommendations.speaker: Upasna Gautam
recommendations.target_audience: Product and technology teams working with editorial staff

recommendations.advice: Define clear key metrics of success and align AI solutions to those metrics - quantify and qualify the trade-off for end users
recommendations.speaker: Upasna Gautam

recommendations.advice: Start stakeholder relationships by asking three questions: What's your biggest goal? What's your biggest pain point? How do you measure success?
recommendations.speaker: Upasna Gautam

recommendations.advice: Focus on one big North Star problem and break it into smaller pieces rather than trying to solve everything at once, especially for smaller newsrooms
recommendations.speaker: Upasna Gautam

recommendations.advice: Build a strong, flexible, modular platform foundation before layering AI capabilities on top
recommendations.speaker: Upasna Gautam

recommendations.advice: Involve editorial teams early in the process and co-design solutions with them - they need to be sponsors, advocates, and teammates
recommendations.speaker: Upasna Gautam

recommendations.advice: Prototype and pilot tools with small, willing teams before scaling to build confidence
recommendations.speaker: Upasna Gautam

recommendations.advice: Prioritize ongoing education about AI's limitations and capabilities so editorial teams feel empowered, not intimidated
recommendations.speaker: Upasna Gautam
